coredata.dc:title: A smart fluorescent colorimetric dual-response sensing for the determination of tetracycline antibiotics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Wen
Wang, Xuchao
Gao, Xun
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: This work synthesized the ethylenediamine-doped puffer fish skin carbon dots using biomass as a green precursor. Three tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) were detected fluorescence and colorimetrically using carbon dots as probes; on this basis, hydrogels were used to carry out visual identification and quantification. In the presence of TCs, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) displayed a quenching impact on fluorescence because of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching, and band gap transition. As a result, it was possible to identify the precise concentrations of tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC). The assay has great biocompatibility, which was supported by cytotoxicity, a wide linear range (0.5–500 M), a low detection limit (0.15 M), and outstanding selectivity. With a high recovery rate, the approach has been successfully used to identify TCs in food and water samples such milk, honey, and tap water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tetracycline antibiotics
Hydrogels
Visualization
Quantitative detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and stability of N-CDs
Assay of TCs in practical samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( YQ202206 ) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( ZQN2016011 ). This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (ProjectNo.:82104349), Jiangsu Provincial Key Discipline Construction Project (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image, (B) The partical diameter distribution, (C) XRD curve, and (D) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.
(A) XPS full scan spectrum of N-CDs, High-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) O1s.

(A) UV absorption spectra (blue line), fluorescence excitation (orange line), and emission spectra (green line) of N-CDs (inset presented images of N-CDs solutions under natural light (left) and UV lamp (right)). (B) Emission spectra of N-CDs at the various excitation wavelengths. (C) Stability of N-CDs impacted by time migration and (D) salinity changes. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of varying concentrations of (A) TC, (C) OTC and (E) CTC; (inset: UV lamp images of varying concentrations of TC, OTC and CTC); plot of the natural logarithm function of F/F0 versus concentrations of (B) TC, (D) OTC and (F) CTC.
Absorbance spectra of varying concentrations of (A) TC, (C) OTC and (E) CTC; relationship between absorbance (at 376/373/390 nm) and the concentration of (B) TC, (D) OTC and (F) CTC.
(A) Effect of distinct ions on N-CDs, (B) Effect of distinct antibiotics on N-CDs.
Normalized color ratio of hydrogel kits as a function of TCs concentration. Inset: images of kits with different concentrations of TCs under UV light.

head.ce:title: A smart fluorescent colorimetric dual-response sensing for the determination of tetracycline antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Marine Bioresources and Environment, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Co-Innovation Center of Jiangsu Marine Bio-Industry Technology, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Co-Innovation Center of Jiangsu Marine Bio-Industry Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Co-Innovation Center of Jiangsu Marine Bio-Industry Technology, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang 222001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016 Shenyang, Liaoning Province, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work synthesized the ethylenediamine-doped puffer fish skin carbon dots using biomass as a green precursor. Three tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) were detected fluorescence and colorimetrically using carbon dots as probes; on this basis, hydrogels were used to carry out visual identification and quantification. In the presence of TCs, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) displayed a quenching impact on fluorescence because of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching, and band gap transition. As a result, it was possible to identify the precise concentrations of tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC). The assay has great biocompatibility, which was supported by cytotoxicity, a wide linear range (0.5–500 M), a low detection limit (0.15 M), and outstanding selectivity. With a high recovery rate, the approach has been successfully used to identify TCs in food and water samples such milk, honey, and tap water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics (TCs), a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics produced by actinomycetes, have been employed to target diseases caused by gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria as well as anaerobic bacteria. However, TC's merits of high broad-spectrum activity and low cost have led to its misuse in various fields such as human health, animal diseases, aquaculture, and agricultural additives, which emerged as the second most abundant antibiotic in the world by volume in 2020 [1]. During metabolism, more than 60 % of TCs are not absorbed, and then they are released into the environment as parent compounds or active metabolites through urinary and fecal excretion, leading to environmental contamination of soil, surface, and groundwater [2]. In addition, the excessive accumulation of tetracycline in the food chain and the presence of residues are potentially hazardous to human health, inducing a series of ailments such as liver and kidney damage, gastrointestinal reactions, endocrine disorders, and defects in the central nervous system [3].

Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC), China, the United States, and the European Union have implemented legislative standards and pharmacopeial guidelines to tightly restrict their residues due to the health risks and environmental damage they are connected with. The CAC has established maximum residue limits (MRLs) for tetracycline in eggs, milk, animal liver and muscle in the range of 100–1200 µg/kg or µg/L [4]. To date, several analytical methods for detecting TCs have been established and reported, such as ultra-high performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (UHPLC–MS) [5], colorimetric [6], photoelectrochemical sensor [7], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) [8], etc. Nevertheless, although these methods are accurate and reliable with good stability, their further application is limited by the need for sample extraction, purification, and other processes, the difficulty of instrument operation, the complexity and time-consuming pre-treatment, the high cost, and the inability to carry out on-site portability. Therefore, it is essential to develop a quick, affordable, visible, and portable TCs residue detection method.

As nanoscience and nanotechnology advance, many kinds of nanomaterials are increasingly being used in fields like sensing, catalysis, biology, and optoelectronics. Due to their superior optical qualities, exceptional biocompatibility, amazing eco-friendliness, and stable tunability, carbon dots (CDs), a revolutionary carbon-based zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted a great deal of scientific interest in recent years [9]. In the process of CDs synthesis, in order to reduce the application of chemical carbon sources and achieve the purpose of simple source, green and sustainable energy, diversified biomass is employed as a raw material to provide carbon sources, such as watermelon rind [10], eggshell membrane [11], fish scale [12], peanut shell [13], sugarcane bagasse [14], etc. Various strategies exist for detecting and differentiating TCs, mostly using CDs as fluorescent probes for fluorescence changes (on–off, on–off-on) between fluorophores and specific targets. Tang et al. generated biomass CDs by simple hydrothermal treatment utilizing carp roe as a precursor for the detection of three tetracyclines (TC, OTC, CTC), for which a limit of detection (LOD) of 41.7 nM [15]; Wang et al. developed highly luminescent nitrogen-doped CDs for the detection of three tetracyclines in serum and milk samples using glucose and ethylenediamine as raw materials, with recoveries ranging from 94.86 % to 109.86 % [16]. Although these methods are sufficiently accurate and reliable to enable the detection and differentiation of multiple tetracycline antibiotics using CDs as fluorescent probes, they still possess limitations in their practical application. Fluorescence detection is a single-mode detection method, and the fluorescence intensity is easily influenced by various external conditions. In addition, due to the larger size of the instrument and the need for professional operation, fluorescence detection is limited in on-site analysis. It is therefore of practical relevance to develop a simple, rapid, visual and portable method for identifying and determining TCs.

A facile method was presented for the synthesis of N-CDs with a high throughput of nitrogen doping by a hydrothermal method employing ethylenediamine and puffer skin as raw materials. TCs may efficiently quench the fluorescence of N-CDs and establish linearity in the range of 0.5–500 L, creating a realistic basis for the quantitative detection of TCs. These effects are mutually regulated by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and band gap transition. Meanwhile, the N-CDs have been implemented for the determination of TCs in complex matrices such as tap water, milk and honey. Compared with other conventional CDs, the new fluorescent sensing material synthesized by this method successfully achieved the detection of three TCs and manifested as indigo, yellow, and blue-violet, respectively under ultraviolet light, which can be directly visually distinguished. And the portable application of detecting, distinguishing, and visualizing TCs was realized by hydrogel.

The synthesis of N-CDs was performed by the one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, the puffer fish skin was dried in the oven and broken into powder. 0.2 g of puffer skin powder and 0.8 mL of C2H8N2 were dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 10 min and then heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 6 h. After allowing the dispersions to cool naturally to room temperature, they were centrifuged at 4200 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was taken and filtered through 0.22 µm micropores to yield N-CDs solution. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain a viscous brown product, which can be used for subsequent experiments.

25 mg of N-CDs solid was dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water to yield the stock solution. 500 μL of different concentrations of TCs (TC, OTC or CTC) were added to 100 μL of N-CDs (2.5 mg/mL), and then diluted to 3000 μL with deionized water. The assay concentrations of TC, OTC and CTC were 0.5 ∼ 500 μM, 0.5 ∼ 500 μM and 2 ∼ 400 μM, respectively. After a thorough mixing vortex of the analytes to be measured and N-CDs, the incubation reaction was carried out under optimal temperature, pH and time conditions. All experiments were performed three times in parallel based on the above.

Milk and honey for TCs assay were purchased from the local supermarket (Benxi, China), and tap water was taken from the laboratory. All three substances underwent sample pretreatment sessions such as centrifugation, filtration, and sedimentation, with the measurement process carried out in triplicate. The milk samples were first mixed with acetonitrile in a ratio of 1:1 by volume and then centrifuged at 13000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was then extracted and filtered to remove the suspended material. 1 g of honey sample was solubilized in 10 mL of deionized water, stirred and sonicated for 10 min. Tap water was centrifuged at 13000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was taken. All the acquired sample supernatants were to be filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membrane and diluted with deionized water at a certain percentage. Three points of 20, 50 and 100 μM were selected for TC, OTC and CTC, respectively, for the computation of the sample recoveries.

The morphology of N-CDs was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). In Fig. 1 A, the prepared N-CDs presented a prominent lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.18 nm, which corresponded to the (101) plane of the graphite lattice spacing [17]. Furthermore, the picture displayed great dispersion while exhibiting no agglomeration characteristics. A statistical analysis of the particle size was performed, the size distribution histogram (Fig. 1A) showed that the particle sizes of N-CDs ranged from 1.32 to 2.28 nm, with an average diameter of 1.90 nm. A clear diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.8° was clearly seen in the X-ray diffractogram (Fig. 1C), which was correlated with the (002) lattice spacing of the graphite [18]. The characterization findings of lattice spacing and diffraction peaks confirmed the existence of graphitic structure and high crystallinity in N-CDs. The surface functional groups of N-CDs were probed by FT-IR (Fig. 1D). The notable absorption peak at 3440 cm−1 was ascribed to the O-H or N-H stretching vibration, the peak at 1640 cm−1 was attributed to the amide group, and the characteristic peak at 1400 cm−1 pertained to the C-N stretching vibration of the amide.

The chemical structure and elemental composition of the N-CDs were further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The entire scan spectra (Fig. 2 A) showed the presence of three primary peaks in the N-CDs at energies of 284.8 eV, 399.78 eV, and 531.25 eV, with respective percentage contents of 68.06 %, 14.26 %, and 17.68 % for the three elements. The C 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2B) can be divided into four peaks: 284.8 eV, 286.13 eV, and 288 eV, which were classified as C-C/C = C, C-O/C-N, and C = O/C = N, respectively [19]. The peaks at 399.55 eV and 401.28 eV in the N 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2C) were fitted and assigned to C-N-C and N-H. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2D) was categorized into 531.19 eV and 532.50 eV, which were linked to C = O and O-H. These data all demonstrated the abundant availability of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of N-CDs, which substantiated the superior hydrophilicity and dispersibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the quest to explore the optical properties of N-CDs, fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were investigated, with the goal of identifying the colors of N-CDs in sunlight and under UV lamps. The picture in Fig. 3 A made it abundantly evident that N-CDs fluoresced blue under UV light while appearing as a clarified translucent pale yellow in sunlight. The shoulder peak in the UV pattern near 280 nm was derived from a π-π* leap in N-CDs, which implied the formation of a conjugate skeleton [20], and the shoulder peak appearing at 315 nm was attributed to a n-π* leap. In addition to above, the maximum excitation at 324 nm and emission at 388 nm regarding N-CDs were also presented in Fig. 3A. In the analysis of the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 3B), it was noticed that the emission peak was red-shifted from 380 nm to 470 nm when the excitation wavelength was gradually varied from 305 nm to 400 nm. The excitation light-dependent property of N-CDs was thereby demonstrated, the phenomenon was differentiated from the excitation-independent property, where the excitation-dependent behavior was mostly induced by the size variation and surface imperfections of nanoparticles themselves. Additionally, it was determined that the QY of N-CDs at 324 nm was 10.31 %.

The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs over time, the concentration of each ion and long-term UV exposure were applied to test their stability. With an experiment cycle every 5 days, it was shown that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs did not significantly vary over the course of one month, illustrating their temporal stability (Fig. 3C). Fig. 3D substantiated the tolerance of N-CDs in a high salt environment, with the fluorescence intensity remaining essentially constant when altering the NaCl concentration from 0 to 2 M. Additionally, N-CDs’ photobleaching resistance was demonstrated by their ability to sustain fluorescence intensity at greater than 95 % of its initial level following 90 min of continuous UV lamp exposure. All of the aforementioned findings supported N-CDs’ excellent stability performance, which offered a dependable priority condition for real-world applications.

To probe the optimal conditions for the synthesis of N-CDs, a detailed analysis of the experimental conditions and reagent content was carried out. The fluorescent intensity of N-CDs varied with the temperature, heating duration, and volume of C2H8N2 supplied, as shown in Fig. S1, suggesting that the synthesis of N-CDs was reliant on these particular parameters. The trend line in the picture, which represented the results of heating the autoclave to 200 °C for six hours while adding 0.8 mL of C2H8N2, illustrated the ideal conditions for the synthesis of N-CDs.

In conducting the experiments, it was discovered that when 400 μM of TC, OTC and CTC solutions were incorporated into the N-CDs, the fluorescence patterns of the N-CDs showed similar changes under identical excitation (324 nm) in the presence of the three TCs, while showing a distinct color differentiation under UV light (Fig. S2). After the addition of these three TCs, the emission wavelength of N-CDs at 388 nm was quenched with a slight red shift; when TC was mixed with N-CDs, the emission light was red-shifted to 423 nm, showing indigo under 365 nm UV irradiation; when OTC and CTC were combined with N-CDs, the emission light was red-shifted to 416 nm, displaying yellow and blue-violet under UV light, respectively.

When establishing the method for the determination of TCs, the corresponding assay conditions were evaluated to ensure the sensitivity and optimal performance of the N-CDs in the actual assay. Fig. S3,4 and 5 indicated the fluorescence attenuation intensity of TC, OTC and CTC on N-CDs at different pH, reaction time and temperature respectively. The optimum detection conditions were determined by the extent to which the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decayed before and after the addition of TCs. Considering that the initial pH of N-CDs was 11, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs reached its maximum in this environment and the TCs also underwent the strongest quenching under the environment. Therefore, the corresponding procedure was simplified, and pH = 11, 0.5 min, and 20 °C were adopted as the optimum conditions for the detection of TCs.

Linear curves were generated for TC, OTC and CTC respectively under optimal experimental conditions. As the TC content increased, the fluorescence intensity fell and the color under the UV lamp progressively changed from light blue to indigo, as illustrated in Fig. 4 A. Good linearity was manifested for 0.5–500 μM: ln(F/F0) = −0.0032[TC]-0.0568, R2 = 0.992, and the LOD was 0.15 μM (S/N = 3). F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 324 nm in the presence and absence of TCs, respectively. Fig. 4C exhibited a progressive color transition from light blue to yellow under UV light with the addition of OTC and a linear relationship of ln(F/F0) = −0.0028[OTC] −0.0154, R2 = 0.992 (0.5–500 μM), the LOD was 0.21 μM (S/N = 3). The response system for CTC was analogous to the described above, with a gradual turn from light blue to blue-violet color accompanied by a linear relationship of ln(F/F0) = −0.0024[CTC]-0.0012, R2 = 0.991 over a concentration range of 2–400 μM, and the LOD was 0.19 μM (S/N = 3). When compared to the relevant regulations, the LOD of TC was found to be lower than the MRL for milk (0.225 μM) as defined by the CAC, US, and EU Food and Drug Administration [21].

The colorimetric response of N-CDs to detect TCs was further explored. The absorption maxima of the mixed system at 376/373/390 nm likewise grew when the concentration of TC/OTC/CTC steadily rose. By linearly fitting the absorbance to the concentration of TCs, a nice linear curve with R2 = 0.998/0.996/0.994 was created (Fig. 5 ). The relative detection limits were 0.19/0.98/0.36 μM (S/N = 3), respectively.

The results confirmed that the present work takes advantage of the dual modes of UV and fluorescence, enabling intuitive detection by watching changes in the colorimetric signal while mutually checking the dependability of the findings, enhancing the realism and credibility of the findings [22].

The possible sensing mechanism of N-CDs for TCs was researched through fluorescence spectrum, UV–vis absorption, and fluorescence lifetime. Firstly, the double exponential fluorescence decay curves were fitted according to Eq.1 [23],
(1) T(t) = A1exp(-t/τ1) + A2exp(-t/τ2)
where A1 and A2 are the corresponding amplitudes, τ1 and τ2 are the time coefficients of the decay curve. The average fluorescence lifetime before and after the addition of TCs were calculated as 1.26 ns, 1.04 ns, 0.71 ns, and 1.09 ns, respectively. It could be observed that the fluorescence lifetime indeed significantly decreased, and it was also noted through Fig. S8 that no new absorption peaks were generated. Therefore, it can be inferred that dynamic quenching occurred. As shown in Fig. S6, the UV absorption spectra of TCs overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching mechanism may have been caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filtering effect (IFE). However, since the fluorescence lifetime was reduced by the addition of the TCs and the FRET mechanism was usually accompanied by a change in fluorescence lifetime, it was inferred that the quenching mechanism is indeed FRET [24].

Furthermore, we found that the fluorescence emission wavelengths of N-CDs were all red-shifted after the addition of TCs (Fig. S2). Further analysis through UV diffuse reflectance spectroscopy revealed that the band gap width decreased from 3.32 eV to 2.81 eV (Fig. S9B), 2.84 eV (Fig. S9C), and 2.65 eV (Fig. S9D). These results indicate that the redshift induced by TCs was caused by band gap transitions [25]. The addition of TCs resulted in structural changes in N-CDs, leading to adjustments in the band positions and band gap widths, consequently impacting the wavelength of fluorescence emission. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs was regulated by FRET, dynamic quenching, and band gap transition together.

Interference and selectivity played a crucial role in establishing the sensing detection platform. The commonly encountered ions, such as cations (Na+, K+, NH4+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ba2+), anions (HCO3−, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, NO2 −) and antibiotics (Cefoxitin, Cefazolin, Cefoperazone, Ampicillin, Azithromycin, Cefixime, Chloramphenicol, Gentamicin, Streptomycin, Mezlocillin, and Sulfadiazine) were taken as reference objects to study the selectivity and interference of this sensing system. While the addition of other reference objects produced nearly unchanged fluorescence readings, the presence of TCs significantly quenched the N-CDs among all the reference materials. Anti-interference experiments were carried out by mixing 1 mM of reference substances with 500 μM of TC. The results were displayed in Fig. 6 , these reference substances did not exert any influence on the fluorescence intensity of the system, and the above results proved that the system had excellent selectivity and resistance to interference. Additionally, the sensing system enabled visual color differentiation while detecting three TCs compared to reported methods, arguably offering a higher detection range and practical significance (Table S1). According to Table S1, it can be clearly observed that, firstly, this experiment detected TCs using a dual detection mode of fluorescence and colorimetry. Compared to traditional single-function sensors, dual-function sensors can replace multiple single-function sensors in the same experiment. By measuring fluorescence signals or color changes, they can detect the presence and concentration of target substances, reducing reagent waste and costs while improving the accuracy and reliability of detection. Secondly, in this experiment, we conducted comprehensive testing of three types of TCs. Although there were multiple analytes, the experiment had a wide detection range and low detection limits. This indicates that the experiment has ability to accurately identify and quantify TCs within a wide concentration range with high sensitivity. Finally, in the actual sample testing, we conducted a comprehensive analysis and detection of tap water, milk, and honey. Compared to other single-sample detections, this multi-sample detection method has significant advantages as it provides more comprehensive information, covering different types of samples, resulting in more accurate and reliable data, further strengthening the credibility of our research conclusions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of TCs in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three samples of tap water, milk, and honey were measured in order to investigate the feasibility of the sensors in real substances. The samples were pretreated and the recoveries and reproducibilities were calculated by adding TCs at different concentrations (20, 50 and 100 μM) by the standard addition method. The assay results for TC, OTC and CTC were presented in Tables S2, S3 and S4 respectively. The recoveries of the three samples ranged from 92.5 % to 116.3 % with a reproducibility (RSD) value of less than 2.90 % (n = 3). These figures all demonstrated the remarkable utility and accuracy of N-CDs in the detection of TCs in practical applications, thus confirming the promising application of the sensor in food and environmental fields.

Furthermore, a portable hydrogel assay kit for the quantitative detection of TCs was constructed by integrating N-CDs in agarose hydrogels, utilizing the image capture capabilities of a smartphone and the image analysis capabilities of ImageJ. The specific procedure of the hydrogel kit was as follows: 0.1 g of agarose was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and stirred and heated at 80 °C for 10 min to present a transparent state, 2 mL of N-CDs solution was added and continued to be heated for 5 min, then 200 μL of the mixed solution was quickly transferred to the cap of a centrifuge tube and left to cool and solidify to obtain the N-CDs hydrogel kit [26]. The steps for the quantitative determination of TCs in the kit were as follows: 100 μL of different concentrations (100,150,200,250,300,350,400,450,500) of TCs were taken and dropped into the kit, respectively, and the reaction was carried out at room temperature for 10 min and then irradiated under a 365 nm UV lamp. The color images of the hydrogels were collected by smartphone and the RGB values in the colors were analyzed through ImageJ software to achieve the quantitative detection of TCs. As Fig. 7 showed, the TCs displayed a well exponential function with the G/B in the RGB values over the concentration range of 100–500 μM, with the equation for TC having G/B = −0.5515e(−[TC] /107.0606) + 0.8968, R2 = 0.997, the LOD was 0.19 μM (S/N = 3). The equation for OTC was G/B = −0.4107e(−[OTC]/166.6119) + 0.8290, R2 = 0.992, the LOD was 0.09 μM (S/N = 3); The formula for CTC was G/B = −0.6436e(−[CTC]/150.8591) + 0.8711, R2 = 0.993, the LOD was 0.37 μM (S/N = 3).

The hydrogel kit's selectivity and stability were also examined experimentally, as previously mentioned. The stability of temperature was firstly examined by storing the hydrogel kits under sealed conditions at 4 °C and 25 °C. The color change and solid content were observed after 48 h and it was found that there was a significant reduction in solids at 25 °C and no change at 4 °C, therefore 4 °C was selected as the storage temperature for the hydrogel kits. Secondly, time stability was examined (Fig. S10). The kits were stored at 4 °C for one week and their color intensity varied within 15 %, with little significant difference in color over time. Finally, selectivity was carried out by adding 1 mM of ions and antibiotics (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cefoxitin, Chloramphenicol, Sulfadiazine, and Sulfathiazole) to the kit. The kit exhibited a clear indigo color in the presence of TC, with a clear color change compared to the blank and interference groups, confirming the excellent selectivity of the kit.

The intelligence of this method lies in its integration of a portable UV lamp, ImageJ software, and data processing programs on smartphones to achieve rapid and accurate quantification analysis of TCs at different concentrations. Firstly, a portable UV lamp was used to obtain the different images produced by the samples irradiated under the UV lamp, and then a smartphone was used for image acquisition to analyze the three different TCs in real-time, which ensured the convenience and sensitivity of the detection process [27]. Subsequently, we converted the acquired optical signals into RGB values by ImageJ software. For colorimetric characteristics, we select the green (G) and blue (B) channels and calculate the ratio to obtain the transformation values [28]. Finally, we compare and calibrate the extracted colorimetric features with pre-established standard curves. By calculating the relevant parameters, the concentrations of different TCs are determined and presented in digital form [29]. In summary, the intelligence of the method is reflected in the fast and accurate analysis of TCs concentration and quantification through image digitization and data processing. This provides a convenient and reliable solution for TCs monitoring and analysis.

Detection of the cytotoxicity of N-CDs was an indicator to evaluate their biocompatibility, therefore the cytotoxicity of N-CDs against three different cell types (HEPAL-6, HEB, HCT116) was measured by the Cell Counting Kit-8 method. Fig. S11, 12 showed that the cells were exposed to different concentrations of N-CDs (12.5–200 μg/mL) and incubated for 48 h. The survival rate of the three cells was still higher than 80 % even at the highest concentration, thus confirming their excellent biocompatibility and laying the foundation for subsequent biomedical applications such as cell imaging, drug delivery and biosensing.

In conclusion, a method for the synthesis of blue fluorescent CDs utilizing puffer fish skin and ethylenediamine was proposed. The benefit of the synthesized N-CDs was that they were extremely stable and immune to environmental impacts, allowing for the quantitative detection of various TC concentrations as well as visual identification by color change under UV light. N-CDs underwent significant fluorescence changes with TCs, and the quenching effect on TCs was mainly achieved through a combination of FRET, band gap reduction, and dynamic quenching. The recoveries ranged from 92.5 %−116.3 %, confirming its reliability and practicality in the detection of food samples. Its excellent biocompatibility was also confirmed by the survival rate of HEPAL-6, HEB and HCT116 cells in the N-CDs solution. A portable, straightforward, inexpensive, instrument- and technician-free hydrogel kit for the visual identification and quantitative detection of TCs was also created using the combination of N-CDs and hydrogels.

Wen Yan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xuchao Wang: Resources, Methodology. Xun Gao: Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011). This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (ProjectNo.:82104349), Jiangsu Provincial Key Discipline Construction Project (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115217.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
